heroOverview: Microphthalmos is a congenital malformation in which one or both eyes are abnormally small, with the total axial length of the affected eye falling at least two standard deviations below the mean for gestational age. The condition occurs in approximately 1–2 per 10,000 live births and accounts for roughly 3–11% of childhood blindness worldwide. The degree of visual impairment ranges from mild reduction in acuity to complete absence of useful vision, depending on whether the eye contains recognizable internal structures (simple microphthalmos) or severe dysplasia (complex microphthalmos). Associated malformations — including coloboma, cataract, and systemic syndromes — are present in more than half of affected individuals. Early identification allows timely socket expansion therapy to guide facial bone growth, preserving cosmesis and preventing orbital asymmetry.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1–2 per 10,000 live births; accounts for 3–11% of global childhood blindness (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; bilateral in roughly 30–40% of cases
keyStats.avgAge: Present at birth; typically detected on newborn examination or prenatal ultrasound
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 19 million children worldwide have some form of childhood visual impairment, with microphthalmos a leading structural cause

definition: Microphthalmos (ICD-10: Q11.2) is a structural malformation of the globe defined by a total axial length more than two standard deviations below the age-adjusted norm — in a term neonate, this means an axial length below approximately 16 mm, compared with the normal 17–18 mm. The condition sits on a phenotypic spectrum alongside clinical anophthalmos (apparent absence of the globe) and colobomatous microphthalmos, in which a persistent embryonic fissure produces a defect through the iris, ciliary body, retina, or optic nerve in combination with reduced globe size. Embryologically, microphthalmos arises from disruption of optic vesicle development, invagination, or secondary degeneration during weeks 4–8 of gestation. The malformation may be isolated (non-syndromic) or part of a syndrome; recognised genetic associations include mutations in SOX2, OTX2, PAX6, STRA6, and FOXE3, among others. Inheritance patterns vary from autosomal dominant and recessive to X-linked and chromosomal. Causative non-genetic factors include first-trimester maternal rubella infection, cytomegalovirus, toxoplasma, varicella, fetal alcohol exposure, vitamin A deficiency, and certain teratogens. Ophthalmologists, medical geneticists, and paediatric ophthalmologists share management responsibility, since up to 60% of cases carry co-occurring ocular or systemic abnormalities.

typesClassification.description: The globe is small but internally organised, with identifiable retina, lens, and optic nerve; visual potential varies but can be meaningful with early correction of refractive errors.
The globe is severely dysplastic — cataract, persistent fetal vasculature, retinal dysplasia, or multiple structural defects coexist with reduced size; vision is typically poor to absent.

Failure of the embryonic fissure to close produces a coloboma affecting iris, choroid, retina, or optic nerve, combined with reduced globe size; visual outcome depends on foveal and optic-nerve involvement.
A subtype of simple microphthalmos with a very short axial length (≤20.5 mm in adults), abnormally thick sclera, and high hyperopia; these eyes are prone to angle-closure glaucoma and uveal effusion.
Globe reduction is one feature of a broader syndrome such as CHARGE, Lenz, or Goltz syndrome; systemic evaluation is mandatory.

primarySymptoms: Visibly small globe on clinical inspection — the affected eye appears substantially smaller than the fellow eye or than age-matched norms, often noticed by parents or on newborn examination.
Reduced or absent vision in the affected eye, ranging from mild acuity reduction in simple forms to light-perception-only or complete blindness in complex disease.

Nystagmus — rhythmic, involuntary eye movements that develop in infancy when severe bilateral visual impairment is present, indicating the visual system has not received adequate input to stabilise gaze.
Strabismus (eye turn) toward or away from the smaller eye, arising because of differing retinal image quality between the two eyes or deficient extraocular muscle development.
High refractive error (marked hyperopia or astigmatism) in the affected eye, which when left uncorrected promotes amblyopia.
Iris or pupil abnormalities, including coloboma (a notch or cleft in the lower iris giving a keyhole appearance), aniridia-like features, or persistent pupillary membranes.
Orbital asymmetry with underdevelopment of the bony orbit on the affected side, which becomes more pronounced if orbital volume expansion is not initiated early.
Eyelid abnormalities, including ptosis (drooping lid), narrowed palpebral fissure, or difficulty fully closing the lids over the small globe.
Photophobia (light sensitivity) in cases with associated anterior segment dysgenesis or cataract.
White pupillary reflex (leukocoria) when a cataract or retinal dysplasia is present within the small globe.
earlyWarningSigns: On the newborn red-reflex test, one eye produces a duller, smaller, or absent reflex compared with the fellow eye.
An infant does not fix and follow faces or lights consistently with one eye when the other is covered.
Parents notice that one eye looks noticeably smaller or set deeper in the socket than the other.
Prenatal ultrasound showing an orbit or globe diameter below the expected range for gestational age.
emergencySigns: Sudden rise in intraocular pressure causing a red, painful, watery eye in a child with nanophthalmos — risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma requiring same-day ophthalmological review.
Rapid onset of white appearance in the pupil (leukocoria) of any infant, which requires urgent examination to exclude retinoblastoma alongside coexisting microphthalmos.
Corneal haziness or large, cloudy cornea in a neonate, suggesting secondary congenital glaucoma needing prompt pressure measurement and treatment.

causes.cause: Genetic mutations (SOX2, OTX2, PAX6, STRA6, FOXE3)
causes.description: Single-gene mutations disrupt transcription factors and signalling proteins essential for optic-cup specification and retinal differentiation. SOX2 mutations, for example, are found in roughly 10–15% of cases of severe bilateral microphthalmos and often co-occur with brain malformations.

Deletions, duplications, or aneuploidy — including trisomies 13 and 18, 4p deletion, and others — disrupt multiple developmental pathways simultaneously, producing microphthalmos as part of a multi-organ syndrome; these account for approximately 10–20% of syndromic cases.

Rubella virus in the first trimester infects the developing lens epithelium and retina, causing cataract, microphthalmos, and retinopathy in up to 50–85% of fetuses infected before 8 weeks; cytomegalovirus and toxoplasma produce choroidal destruction and secondary globe atrophy.

causes.cause: Vitamin A deficiency or STRA6 dysfunction
causes.description: Retinoic acid signalling is essential for optic vesicle patterning; mutations in STRA6 (the cellular receptor for retinol-binding protein) or severe maternal vitamin A deficiency disrupt this pathway, causing microphthalmos often combined with diaphragmatic hernia and mental disability (Matthew-Wood syndrome).

Fetal alcohol syndrome, thalidomide, warfarin, and certain anticonvulsants used in early pregnancy each interfere with neural-crest and neuroectodermal migration, with ocular malformation risk highest during gestational weeks 4–8.
In approximately 30–40% of isolated cases no causative genetic variant or environmental trigger is identified; non-penetrant germline variants or somatic mosaicism are suspected contributors.

riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with anophthalmos, microphthalmos, or coloboma raises the prior probability of a heritable gene variant; genetic counselling is indicated for all affected families.
First- or second-cousin parentage increases the chance of autosomal-recessive causes such as STRA6 or FOXE3 mutations, raising recurrence risk to approximately 25% per pregnancy.

riskFactors.factor: First-trimester rubella infection in the mother
riskFactors.description: Maternal rubella before 8 weeks of gestation carries the highest teratogenic risk, affecting the lens and retinal vasculature; unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated mothers are at risk.
Severe deficiency in low-resource settings disrupts retinoic acid signalling essential for optic vesicle formation, contributing to preventable cases of structural ocular malformation.
Heavy alcohol use in early pregnancy, or use of known ocular teratogens such as high-dose vitamin A derivatives (isotretinoin), warfarin, or carbamazepine, increases the risk of eye malformation.

riskFactors.factor: Chromosomal or syndromic diagnosis in the fetus
riskFactors.description: Conditions such as trisomy 13, CHARGE syndrome, or Lenz syndrome carry substantially elevated rates of microphthalmos, detectable by prenatal chromosomal microarray.
Very-low-birthweight infants have a higher rate of structural eye malformations alongside retinopathy of prematurity; premature retinal vascularisation may be an additional risk.

affectedDemographics: Infants in countries where rubella vaccination coverage is incomplete
Children in low-resource settings with high rates of maternal nutritional deficiency
Neonates with trisomy 13 or 18 (where eye malformations occur in >30% of cases)
Families with autosomal-recessive gene variants (higher rate with consanguinity)
diagnosisOverview: Microphthalmos is often detected on prenatal ultrasound after 20 weeks when the orbital diameter falls below gestational-age norms, or by a neonatologist observing an asymmetrically small globe on the routine newborn examination. The cornerstone of postnatal assessment is formal ophthalmic evaluation including B-scan ultrasonography to measure axial length and define internal globe structure, slit-lamp examination to document anterior-segment anatomy, dilated fundoscopy to assess the retina and optic nerve, and cycloplegic refraction to quantify refractive error. Intraocular pressure measurement is essential in all cases because associated glaucoma occurs in up to 15% of microphthalmic eyes. Once the ocular phenotype is established, the workup broadens to systemic evaluation: paediatric cardiology for structural heart defects, hearing assessment, renal ultrasound, and brain MRI (relevant given central nervous system associations with SOX2 and OTX2 mutations). Genetic investigation — starting with chromosomal microarray and a targeted gene panel or exome sequencing — is recommended for all bilateral cases and for unilateral cases with additional systemic findings or a family history. Electroretinography assesses retinal function and guides prognosis. Key differential diagnoses are clinical anophthalmos (apparent globe absence) and phthisis bulbi (a shrunken, degenerated globe that had normal early development).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures axial globe length and identifies internal structures such as retinal folds, cataract, vitreous haemorrhage, or persistent fetal vasculature.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel is applied to the closed eyelid and a probe is gently moved across it; the procedure is painless and takes about 10 minutes in cooperative children.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the refractive error that drives amblyopia and documents anterior-segment anatomy including iris, cornea, and lens.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Eye drops are used to dilate the pupils; a slit-lamp is a specialised microscope the clinician uses to view the front of the eye while the child rests chin and forehead on a support.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises the retina, optic nerve, and choroid for coloboma, retinal dysplasia, or hypoplasia that predicts visual potential.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After dilation drops, the ophthalmologist uses a bright handheld or headlight-mounted lens to examine the back of the eye; it may take 30–45 minutes and temporarily blurs near vision.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies copy-number variants and single-gene mutations in SOX2, OTX2, PAX6, STRA6, and other causative genes, informing recurrence risk and systemic co-morbidity screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is taken from a peripheral vein; results typically take 4–8 weeks from a clinical genetics laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects associated structural brain abnormalities (e.g. septo-optic dysplasia, cortical malformations with SOX2 mutations) and precisely characterises orbital volume to guide socket expansion planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive scan lasting 30–60 minutes; infants often need a short general anaesthetic or feed-and-wrap technique to remain still.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures electrical responses of the retinal photoreceptors to determine whether the retina is functional, informing prognosis for visual development.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the cornea under topical anaesthesia and on the forehead; the child watches flashing lights; typically performed under sedation in very young children.

treatmentOverview: Management of microphthalmos is tailored to the degree of globe underdevelopment and whether the eye has any visual potential. For eyes with useful vision, the primary goal is treating amblyopia: spectacles or contact lenses correct refractive error and patching of the fellow eye stimulates the affected eye during the critical period of visual development (ages 0–8). Intraocular pressure is monitored and treated medically or surgically if glaucoma develops. For cosmetically significant microphthalmos, regardless of visual potential, graduated socket expansion is the cornerstone of treatment. Custom-fitted conformers (acrylic shells placed in the socket) are progressively enlarged over months to years, mechanically stimulating orbital bone growth; without expansion, the orbit and midface on the affected side will remain smaller, producing permanent facial asymmetry. In older children, surgery may reconstruct the socket further, and a prosthetic shell or ocular prosthesis can provide an excellent cosmetic outcome. Systemic abnormalities identified through the genetic workup — congenital heart defects, endocrine dysfunction, CNS malformations — are addressed by the relevant specialists. Genetic counselling is offered to the family to clarify inheritance and recurrence risk. Multidisciplinary teams comprising ophthalmology, paediatrics, medical genetics, ocularistry, and low-vision rehabilitation provide the best outcomes. Cochrane reviews have not identified a single superior socket expansion protocol, and management is individualised to the child's rate of orbital growth and tolerance of conformer wear.

medicalTreatments.name: Spectacles or contact lens correction
medicalTreatments.description: High hyperopia and astigmatism in the microphthalmic eye are corrected with spectacles or rigid gas-permeable contact lenses as early as the first months of life, reducing the refractive amblyopia that compounds structural visual loss.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In simple microphthalmos with a formed retina, early optical correction combined with patching improves acuity in approximately 40–60% of affected eyes according to cohort studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Occlusion (patching) therapy
medicalTreatments.description: The better-seeing fellow eye is patched for several hours daily to force the visual cortex to process input from the microphthalmic eye, counteracting amblyopia during the sensitive period of visual development.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves visual acuity by at least 2 logMAR lines in 40–60% of children with simple microphthalmos when initiated before age 8.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical glaucoma medications (prostaglandin analogues, beta-blockers, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors)
medicalTreatments.description: When intraocular pressure is elevated in the microphthalmic eye — particularly in nanophthalmos — topical drops reduce aqueous production or increase outflow, protecting residual retinal ganglion cells.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces intraocular pressure by an average of 20–30% depending on agent; combination therapy achieves control in approximately 60–70% of cases without surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Graduated conformer socket expansion
medicalTreatments.description: Custom acrylic conformers or tissue expanders of progressively larger volumes are inserted into the socket, providing mechanical stimulation to guide orbital bone growth; replaced every 4–6 weeks throughout infancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces orbital volume asymmetry by 50–70% compared with non-expanded controls in prospective series; early initiation before 6 months gives the best results.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical dermis-fat graft or implant placement
medicalTreatments.description: In children who cannot tolerate serial conformers, or in older children with established orbital asymmetry, autologous dermis-fat grafts or porous polyethylene implants augment orbital volume.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores orbital symmetry in approximately 70–80% of cases in retrospective surgical series, though re-operation for graft atrophy is needed in 10–20%.

surgicalOptions.name: Trabeculectomy or goniotomy for secondary glaucoma
surgicalOptions.description: When topical medications fail to control intraocular pressure in a nanophthalmic or complex microphthalmic eye, filtering surgery or goniotomy reduces pressure to protect any remaining vision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 60–70% of cases achieve target pressure after a single procedure at 1 year; re-operation is required in roughly 20–30% over 5 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Cataract extraction
surgicalOptions.description: When a visually significant cataract is present inside the small globe and the retina is assessable, early surgery with optical rehabilitation can unlock visual potential; the decision must weigh the risks of surgery in a dysplastic eye against possible visual gain.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual improvement reported in 30–50% of carefully selected cases with formed retina; higher complication rate than in normal-sized eyes.

surgicalOptions.name: Ocular prosthesis fitting
surgicalOptions.description: A custom-painted ocular shell or prosthesis provides cosmetic rehabilitation for sightless or near-sightless eyes; the prosthesis sits over the globe or in an expanded socket and is resized periodically as the orbit grows.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves cosmetically acceptable symmetry in approximately 85–90% of cases when socket expansion has been adequate; patient and family satisfaction is high in long-term studies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-vision rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: For children with residual vision, certified orientation and mobility specialists and low-vision therapists use magnifiers, tactile aids, and specialist lighting to maximise functional independence.

alternativeTreatments.name: Genetic counselling
alternativeTreatments.description: Pre-conception and prenatal genetic counselling helps families with a known causative variant understand recurrence risk (up to 50% for dominant causes) and the availability of preimplantation genetic testing.

whySeeSpecialist: Microphthalmos requires co-ordinated assessment by a paediatric ophthalmologist and a clinical geneticist from diagnosis. The ophthalmologist defines visual potential, manages amblyopia and glaucoma, and oversees socket expansion, while the geneticist interprets molecular findings, screens for systemic co-morbidities, and guides family planning. Delayed referral beyond the first weeks of life reduces the window for amblyopia treatment and allows orbital asymmetry to become established.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric ophthalmologist at a centre with experience in childhood structural eye malformations and dedicated ocularistry services for custom conformer fitting. A clinical genetics team with access to chromosomal microarray and exome sequencing is essential for bilateral or syndromic cases. Centres affiliated with teaching hospitals often have multidisciplinary craniofacial teams able to address associated orbital and midface asymmetry.
preventionStrategies: Ensure rubella vaccination is up to date before pregnancy to prevent first-trimester fetal infection.
Avoid known ocular teratogens — including isotretinoin, warfarin, and high-dose alcohol — during the periconceptional period and first trimester.
Supplement folic acid and maintain adequate nutritional status, including vitamin A, in pregnancy.
Seek genetic counselling before a subsequent pregnancy when microphthalmos has been diagnosed in a child or close relative.
Accept prenatal chromosomal microarray or genetic testing when a fetal orbital anomaly is detected on ultrasound.
lifestyleModifications: Comply with daily conformer or prosthesis wear to maintain socket expansion and comfort.
Attend all follow-up appointments for intraocular pressure monitoring and optical correction updates.
Protect the better-seeing eye with polycarbonate safety spectacles during sports and activities.
Inform educators about the child's visual limitations to ensure appropriate classroom seating and visual aids.
Maintain good hygiene around the prosthesis to prevent socket infection.

recommended: A balanced diet adequate in vitamin A (from liver, dairy, orange and yellow vegetables) supports general ocular health
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or algal supplements, which support retinal photoreceptor membrane integrity
Iron-adequate nutrition to prevent anaemia, which in infancy can worsen visual inattentiveness
avoid: Vitamin A megadosing during pregnancy, which paradoxically can cause teratogenic ocular malformations
Alcohol in pregnancy, particularly in the first trimester when ocular organogenesis is active

exerciseGuidelines: Children with microphthalmos should participate fully in physical activity. When only one eye has useful vision, protective polycarbonate spectacles are mandatory for all contact sports and activities with eye-injury risk. Orientation and mobility training helps children with bilateral visual impairment develop confidence in navigating their environment safely.
dailyManagement: Insert, clean, and remove the conformer or prosthesis as instructed by the ocularist, typically removing it at night for cleaning.
Apply prescribed patching therapy for the recommended daily hours and keep a log to show at ophthalmology visits.
Administer topical glaucoma drops on schedule if prescribed; set phone reminders for consistency.
Report any pain, redness, discharge, or sudden change in vision in either eye promptly to the ophthalmologist.
Ensure the child wears the prescribed spectacles or contact lenses at all waking hours to prevent amblyopia deepening.
prognosis: The visual prognosis of microphthalmos is strongly determined by the severity of structural dysplasia and whether the fovea and optic nerve are formed. In simple microphthalmos with a relatively intact retina, aggressive amblyopia therapy initiated in early infancy can yield functional vision in 40–60% of affected eyes. Complex or colobomatous microphthalmos with macular involvement more often results in severe visual impairment or blindness in the affected eye. When the condition is bilateral, the likelihood of requiring lifelong low-vision or blindness support is considerably higher. Socket expansion therapy, regardless of visual outcome, reliably improves cosmetic symmetry. With appropriate rehabilitation and educational support, children with visual impairment from microphthalmos achieve educational milestones and lead productive lives. Regular long-term follow-up is needed because glaucoma can develop or progress at any age and visual aids need updating as the child grows.
recoveryTimeline: Socket expansion is a multi-year process: conformers are typically replaced every 4–6 weeks in infancy, then every 3–6 months as growth slows; most of the orbital asymmetry correction is achieved by age 5–7. Amblyopia therapy is most effective when started before age 3 and is usually continued until visual maturity around age 8–10. If surgical interventions for glaucoma or cataract are performed, in-patient stays are typically 1–2 days and return to normal activity occurs within 2 weeks.
complications: Glaucoma — elevated intraocular pressure occurs in up to 15% of microphthalmic eyes, particularly nanophthalmos, and requires lifelong monitoring and treatment.
Irreversible amblyopia — failure to treat refractive error and prescribe patching before the critical period (age 8) leaves the visual cortex permanently under-developed for the affected eye.

Orbital asymmetry and midface hypoplasia — if socket expansion is not started early, the bony orbit fails to grow to normal volume, producing lasting facial asymmetry that becomes harder to correct with age.
Retinal detachment — the structurally abnormal retina in complex microphthalmos is at elevated risk of detachment; symptoms include sudden new floaters or a shadow across vision.
Angle-closure glaucoma in nanophthalmos — the shallow anterior chamber can precipitate acute angle closure, particularly after procedures that change lens position or anterior chamber depth.
Psychological and social impact — children with visible ocular asymmetry are at increased risk of bullying and low self-esteem; proactive psychological support and peer connection improve outcomes.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Anophthalmia and Microphthalmia
supportResources.description: NIH patient guide on structural eye malformations covering causes, diagnosis, and support resources.

supportResources.name: Aniridia Foundation International — Structural Eye Conditions
supportResources.description: Patient organisation providing support and information for families affected by structural ocular malformations.

supportResources.name: NHS — Microphthalmia, Anophthalmia, and Coloboma
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on microphthalmos, its assessment, and socket expansion therapy for families.

sources.title: Verma AS, FitzPatrick DR. Anophthalmia and microphthalmia. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases, 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A congenital cataract produces a white or opaque pupil in a globe of normal size, whereas microphthalmos is defined by a structurally reduced globe diameter; both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital glaucoma causes an enlarged, cloudy cornea and raised intraocular pressure, the opposite of the small globe in microphthalmos — though nanophthalmos can produce secondary glaucoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iris coloboma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A coloboma can occur in a normally sized globe; colobomatous microphthalmos has the iris notch combined with a globe that is abnormally small by axial-length measurement.

relatedConditions.name: Macrophthalmos
relatedConditions.relevance: The complementary condition of pathologically enlarged globe, sharing clinical genetics assessment and ophthalmic surveillance pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary glaucoma is a recognised complication of microphthalmos, particularly in the nanophthalmic subtype, requiring shared monitoring.

relatedConditions.name: Coloboma of the lens
relatedConditions.relevance: Lens coloboma frequently accompanies colobomatous microphthalmos and contributes to refractive error and visual impairment.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Marfan syndrome causes lens dislocation and high myopia; in the differential for syndromic structural eye malformations.

relatedConditions.name: Down syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Trisomy 21 increases the prevalence of structural eye malformations and requires routine ophthalmic surveillance.

faqs.question: What is microphthalmos?
faqs.answer: Microphthalmos means one or both eyes are abnormally small from birth. The globe measures more than two standard deviations below the normal length for age. Depending on how the eye formed internally, vision ranges from mild reduction to complete blindness.

faqs.question: Is microphthalmos the same as anophthalmia?
faqs.answer: No. Anophthalmia is the apparent absence of the globe; microphthalmos means the eye is present but undersized. They sit on the same developmental spectrum and the terms are sometimes used loosely, but they differ by the amount of residual eye tissue.

faqs.question: Can a child with microphthalmos see?
faqs.answer: It depends on the subtype. In simple microphthalmos with a relatively intact retina, early spectacle correction and patching therapy can produce useful vision in 40–60% of affected eyes. Complex or colobomatous disease with macular involvement more often results in severe impairment or blindness.

faqs.question: What causes microphthalmos?
faqs.answer: Causes include gene mutations (SOX2, OTX2, PAX6), chromosomal abnormalities, first-trimester rubella or CMV infection, vitamin A deficiency, and exposure to teratogens such as alcohol or isotretinoin in early pregnancy. In roughly 30–40% of isolated cases no cause is found.

faqs.question: Is microphthalmos genetic?
faqs.answer: It can be. Approximately 50–60% of bilateral cases have an identifiable genetic cause, including single-gene mutations and chromosomal abnormalities. Families should be offered genetic counselling and testing to understand inheritance and recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What is a conformer and why is it used?
faqs.answer: A conformer is a custom-made acrylic shell placed inside the socket to mechanically expand the orbit. Because bony orbital growth follows the contained tissue volume, progressively larger conformers guide the orbit to grow toward normal size, reducing facial asymmetry.

faqs.question: When should socket expansion begin?
faqs.answer: Ideally within the first weeks to months of life, when orbital bone is most responsive to mechanical stimulation. Earlier initiation consistently produces better orbital symmetry outcomes in prospective series. Referral to an ocularist should be made at the time of diagnosis.

faqs.question: Does microphthalmos affect the other eye?
faqs.answer: In unilateral cases the fellow eye is typically normal and provides good vision. It should be protected with polycarbonate safety spectacles during activities with eye-injury risk, since it is the sole source of useful sight. Any concern about the fellow eye — refractive error, cataract, or strabismus — should be addressed promptly.

faqs.question: What tests are needed to diagnose microphthalmos?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires ophthalmic examination with B-scan ultrasound to measure axial length and inspect internal structure, cycloplegic refraction, dilated funduscopy, and intraocular pressure measurement. Chromosomal microarray and gene-panel sequencing are recommended for bilateral or syndromic cases.

faqs.question: Can microphthalmos be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prenatal ultrasound from about 20 weeks can show orbital diameters below the expected range for gestational age. Fetal MRI provides more detailed assessment when ultrasound is uncertain. Genetic testing of amniotic fluid or chorionic villi can identify chromosomal causes.

faqs.question: What is nanophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Nanophthalmos is a subtype of simple microphthalmos with an axial length below 20.5 mm in an adult, abnormally thick sclera, and very high hyperopia. These eyes are structurally well formed but prone to angle-closure glaucoma and uveal effusion, which require specific surveillance and management.

faqs.question: Will my child need surgery?
faqs.answer: Not all children need intraocular surgery. Conformer socket expansion is non-surgical and is needed in most cases with significant cosmetic asymmetry. Surgical intervention is reserved for specific indications: glaucoma uncontrolled by drops, a visually significant cataract in a formed retina, or orbital reconstruction in older children.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk for future pregnancies?
faqs.answer: Recurrence risk depends on the cause. Autosomal-recessive gene variants carry a 25% risk per pregnancy. Autosomal-dominant variants carry a 50% risk. Sporadic chromosomal errors carry a lower but slightly elevated recurrence risk. A clinical geneticist can calculate the specific risk once the cause is identified.

faqs.question: Does microphthalmos get worse over time?
faqs.answer: The globe itself does not grow worse, but secondary complications — glaucoma, retinal detachment, orbital asymmetry — can progress if left untreated. Regular monitoring for intraocular pressure and visual development is needed throughout childhood and into adulthood.

faqs.question: How is amblyopia treated in microphthalmos?
faqs.answer: Spectacle or contact lens correction addresses the refractive component of amblyopia, and patching of the fellow eye forces the visual cortex to use the affected eye. Therapy is most effective when started as early as possible in infancy and continued until visual maturity around age 8–10.

faqs.question: Can microphthalmos be associated with brain problems?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly when caused by SOX2 or OTX2 mutations, which also control brain development. Associated findings can include septo-optic dysplasia, corpus callosum anomalies, or pituitary hormone deficiencies. Brain MRI is part of the standard workup for bilateral cases.

faqs.question: Is a prosthetic eye used in microphthalmos?
faqs.answer: In cosmetically significant cases — particularly sightless eyes — a custom-painted ocular shell prosthesis fits over the globe or in the expanded socket to match the fellow eye. Prostheses are resized periodically as the orbit grows and achieve excellent cosmesis in most cases.

faqs.question: What support is available for families?
faqs.answer: The National Eye Institute, NHS, and patient advocacy organisations such as the Vision for Tomorrow Foundation provide information, peer support, and guidance on accessing low-vision services and educational accommodations for children with visual impairment.

faqs.question: Can microphthalmos affect hearing or other organs?
faqs.answer: In syndromic forms — such as CHARGE syndrome or branchio-oto-renal syndrome — microphthalmos co-occurs with hearing loss and kidney malformations. A full systemic review by a paediatrician and clinical geneticist is standard in bilateral or syndromic cases.

faqs.question: Will my child be able to attend mainstream school?
faqs.answer: Most children with unilateral microphthalmos attend mainstream school with standard support. Children with bilateral severe visual impairment benefit from specialist visual rehabilitation, assistive technology, and an individualised education plan, enabling full participation in mainstream or specialist settings.

metaDescription: Microphthalmos is a congenital small-eye malformation causing visual impairment. Learn causes, diagnosis, socket expansion and treatment options.
